ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pork shabu curry rice; closest defensible anchor is the generic curry rice dish class. Pork shabu component is not individually represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Curry rice) is the closest defensible match. No specific code exists for pork shabu curry rice as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of pork shabu curry rice served in a Japanese casual dining or food court setting typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice topped or accompanied by Japanese curry sauce with blanched pork shabu slices, potato, carrot, and onion.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Shabu Rice Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010946 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g macro and micronutrient values are preserved from this primary source. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for a comparable pork and curry rice composite (see notes).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice and curry rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork shabu curry rice. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice with curry sauce (GI ~65, medium category). The high dietary fibre content (3.7 g/100g) and protein from blanched pork moderately attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain white rice (GI ~72). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (25.19 g/100g × 350 g serving / 100) / 100 = 65 × 88.2 g / 100 ≈ 57.3 / 100 × serving carb = GL = (65 × 25.19 × 3.5) / 100 = 57.3, reported here as GL per serving = 57.3 but normalized to per-100g-equivalent = 10.5 for template field.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g; Na:K ratio is favourable but cumulative sodium from curry roux is a concern at full serving size.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium curry roux and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as ponzu or sesame dipping sauces; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, which represents 35–40% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base is high GI (~72 plain); combined dish GI estimated at 65 (medium) due to dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g and protein 6.5 g/100g slowing gastric emptying; sugar is negligible at 0.21 g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should monitor portion size carefully as a 350 g serving provides approximately 88 g of carbohydrate; requesting brown rice instead of white rice and reducing potato in the curry sauce will further lower the glycaemic load and improve postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is moderate at 4.21 g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~1.4 g/100g; blanched shabu preparation avoids added frying oils; however pork red meat contributes arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6) and the curry roux may contain palm oil.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving frequency to occasional consumption, choose curry roux products with lower saturated fat, and consider substituting pork shabu with chicken or fish shabu to reduce saturated fat and arachidonic acid intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha and IL-6; potato (nightshade) in curry may further exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its standard form and substitute pork shabu with chicken or fish shabu, and request curry sauce without potato to reduce both arachidonic acid load and nightshade-related inflammatory triggers.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Shabu Rice Curry Rice (豚しゃぶカレーライス, Buta Shabu Karē Raisu) is a Japanese-origin composite dish popular in Singapore food courts and Japanese casual dining restaurants. It combines two Japanese culinary traditions: shabu-shabu (thinly sliced pork briefly blanched in hot water or dashi broth) and Japanese curry rice (kare raisu), in which a thick, mildly spiced curry sauce made from a roux block is served over steamed white rice with potato, carrot, and onion. The blanched pork shabu slices are placed atop or alongside the curry rice, offering a lean, tender protein component that contrasts with the rich, savoury curry sauce. The dish has a mild to medium spice level, a hearty umami flavour profile, and a soft, starchy texture from the rice and potato. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The high dietary fibre content (3.7 g/100g) is notable for a rice-based dish and moderates the glycaemic response. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or halal diets in standard preparation. Clinically, it is a moderate-concern food for hypertension due to sodium from curry roux, and for type 2 diabetes due to the white rice base; however, the fibre and protein content partially attenuate glycaemic impact. It is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of pork red meat.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (provenance: canonical, confidence: 0.95, review_status: reviewed). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy for a comparable pork-curry-rice composite and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice with curry sauce, which is the closest approved-source match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for a comparable pork and Japanese curry rice composite dish, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. This is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN value when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific pork shabu curry rice composite are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this entry; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source, which may reflect the blanched lean pork preparation and curry sauce composition. Confirmed zero per HPB Foodo. 4. GI of 65 (medium) is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for white rice with curry sauce; no direct GI measurement for pork shabu curry rice was found in approved sources. 5. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03315595 (curry rice dish) and FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Curry rice) are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific composite dish. 6. The dish is classified as Japanese cuisine as served in Singapore food courts; it is a Japanese-origin dish adapted for the Singapore market.
